content:
# Acts Chapter 14 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 242-243 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_014_page_001.jpg, ACT_014_page_002.jpg

---

**1** In Iconium the same thing happened: Paul and Barnabas went into the Jewish synagogue and spoke so powerfully that a great number of both Jews and Greeks came to faith.

**2** But the Jews who refused to believe stirred up the Gentiles and poisoned their minds against the brothers.

**3** So Paul and Barnabas stayed on for a considerable time, speaking boldly for the Lord, who confirmed the message of his grace by enabling them to perform signs and wonders.

**4** The people of the city were split — some siding with the Jews, others with the apostles.

**5** When a plot was hatched by both Gentiles and Jews, together with their leaders, to abuse and stone them,

**6** they found out about it and escaped to the Lycaonian cities of Lystra and Derbe and the surrounding region.

**7** And there they continued to spread the good news.

**8** Now in Lystra there was a man sitting who had no strength in his feet. He had been lame from birth — he had never walked a step in his life.

**9** This man was listening to Paul speak. Paul looked straight at him, saw that he had faith to be healed,

**10** and called out in a loud voice, "Stand up on your feet!" The man jumped up and began to walk.

**11** When the crowds saw what Paul had done, they shouted in the Lycaonian language, "The gods have come down to us in human form!"

**12** They called Barnabas "Zeus" and Paul "Hermes," because he was the one who did the talking.

**13** The priest of Zeus, whose temple stood just outside the city, brought bulls and garlands to the gates, and he and the crowds wanted to offer sacrifices to them.

**14** But when the apostles Barnabas and Paul heard about this, they tore their clothes, rushed out into the crowd, and shouted,

**15** "Friends, why are you doing this? We are only human beings, just like you! We are here to bring you good news — to turn you away from these worthless things and toward the living God, who made heaven and earth and the sea and everything in them.

**16** In past generations he let all the nations go their own way.

**17** Yet he has not left himself without evidence of who he is: he shows kindness by giving you rain from heaven and fruitful seasons, filling your hearts with food and gladness."

**18** Even with these words, they barely managed to stop the crowds from sacrificing to them.

**19** Then Jews arrived from Antioch and Iconium and won the crowds over. They stoned Paul and dragged him outside the city, leaving him for dead.

**20** But when the disciples gathered around him, he got up and went back into the city. The next day he left with Barnabas for Derbe.

**21** After proclaiming the good news in that city and making a substantial number of disciples, they retraced their steps to Lystra, then to Iconium, and then to Antioch,

**22** strengthening the souls of the disciples and encouraging them to hold firm in the faith. "We must pass through many hardships," they said, "to enter the kingdom of God."

**23** In each church they appointed elders, and with prayer and fasting they entrusted them to the Lord in whom they had put their faith.

**24** Then they passed through Pisidia and came to Pamphylia.

**25** After speaking the message in Perga, they went down to Attalia.

**26** From there they sailed back to Antioch — the very place where they had been entrusted to God's grace for the work they had now completed.

**27** When they arrived, they gathered the church together and reported everything God had done through them, and how he had opened a door of faith for the Gentiles.

**28** And they stayed there with the disciples for a considerable time.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 2 — "poisoned their minds":** The Greek (ekakosan) carries the sense of making evil or hostile; "poisoned their minds" captures the active malice of the campaign against the believers more vividly than a literal "made evil."

**Verse 4 — "split":** The Sinaiticus reading has the city divided (eschisthe). The sharp, monosyllabic "split" conveys the sudden factional rupture Luke describes.

**Verse 8 — "never walked a step in his life":** This expands the Greek (hos oudepote periepatesen) for dramatic emphasis. Luke is building the case for the magnitude of the miracle — the man's condition was total and lifelong.

**Verse 11 — "The gods have come down to us in human form":** The Lycaonian crowd's native reaction reveals how deeply embedded polytheistic assumption was. They did not reach for Jewish categories; they reached for their own. The phrase homoiothentes anthropois ("having been made like men") is rendered dynamically to capture the crowd's awe.

**Verse 12 — Zeus and Hermes:** Barnabas received the higher title, Zeus, perhaps because of his more commanding physical presence. Paul, as the chief speaker (ho hegoumenos tou logou — "the leader of the word"), was identified with Hermes, the divine messenger. This is one of the most revealing cultural-collision scenes in Acts.

**Verse 15 — "worthless things":** The Greek mataioi ("vain, empty, futile") describes the idols and their worship. "Worthless" carries the dismissive force Paul and Barnabas intended — these so-called gods are nothing.

**Verse 17 — "evidence of who he is":** The Greek amarturon ("without witness") is rendered as "evidence of who he is" to make clear that God's self-testimony is what is at stake — creation itself testifies.

**Verse 19 — The stoning of Paul:** The abrupt reversal is stunning. The same crowds that wanted to worship Paul as a god are now persuaded to stone him. Luke narrates this without commentary, letting the whiplash speak for itself. The Sinaiticus text reads consistent with the majority here.

**Verse 22 — "We must pass through many hardships":** The Greek (dia pollon thlipseon) uses thlipsis — pressure, affliction, crushing weight. "Hardships" in context captures that this is not minor inconvenience but real suffering, as Paul's own stoning has just demonstrated. The teaching is embodied in the teacher.

**Verse 26 — "the very place where they had been entrusted to God's grace":** This is a structural bookend. Luke closes the first missionary journey by returning to its point of origin (cf. 13:1-3), completing the narrative arc. The word paradedomenoi ("having been handed over, entrusted") echoes the commissioning scene.

— PNEUMA
